Name: information_theoretic_neural_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the compressibility of neural networks is fundamentally limited by the rate-distortion tradeoff, and that this limit can be quantified using information-theoretic measures such as mutual information and entropy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Geometric Signal Separation have explored the use of geometric and information-theoretic methods for neural network compression and pruning. However, these works do not provide a fundamental limit on the compressibility of neural networks. Our work aims to fill this gap by deriving information-theoretic bounds on the compressibility of neural networks using rate-distortion theory.
Abstract: Neural network compression is a crucial technique for reducing the computational requirements of deep learning models. However, the fundamental limits of neural network compression are not well understood. In this work, we propose a rate-distortion approach to neural network compression, which quantifies the tradeoff between the compression rate and the resulting distortion. We derive information-theoretic bounds on the compressibility of neural networks using mutual information and entropy, and demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several benchmark datasets. Our results provide a fundamental limit on the compressibility of neural networks, and have important implications for the design of efficient compression algorithms.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on several benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10, using a variety of neural network architectures. We will evaluate the performance of our rate-distortion approach using metrics such as peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) and multi-scale structural similarity (MS-SSIM). We will also compare our results to existing compression algorithms, such as quantization and pruning, to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that our approach may not be applicable to all types of neural networks, such as those with complex or non-differentiable activation functions. Additionally, our approach may require significant computational resources to derive the information-theoretic bounds, which could limit its practical applicability.
